content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
November 9,
1993
Debra A. Greenfield
General Counsel
San Diego Association of
Governments
Suite 800, First Interstate Plaza
401
“B” Street
San Diego,
CA 92101
Re:
Your Request for Formal Advice
Our File No.
A-93-347
Dear Ms. Greenfield:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Mike Bixler,
a member of the board of directors of the
San Diego Association of Governments
(“SANDAG”)
and mayor of the
City of Imperial Beach,
regarding his responsibilities under the
conflict—of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
“Act”).
1
This letter will also confirm our telephone conversation
of October 21,
1992.
QUESTION
The Regional Transportation Plan
(“RTP”)
for the San Diego
region contains an aviation element providing for the
consideration of aviation sites other than Lindbergh Field.
Mayor
Bixler has three real property interests located in the airport
influence area of Lindbergh Field that will be financially
affected by these decisions.
May Mayor Bixier participate in
S.ANDAG’s decisions concerning the RTP?
CONCLUSION
Mayor Bixier may participate in SANDAG’s decisions concerning
the RTP if the effect of those decisions on his real property
interests will be substantially the same as the effect upon at
least 25 percent of all the properties which are located within a
2,500 foot radius of the boundaries of his properties,
or there
are at least 10 properties under separate ownership within a 2,500
foot radius of Mayor Bixier’s properties.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A—93—347
Page
2
FACTS
SANDAG,
a joint powers authority composed of
18 cities and
the County of San Diego,
serves as the Regional Transportation
Planning Agency
(“RTPA”)
for the San Diego region.
As the RTPA,
SANDAG is responsible for development and adoption of the Regional
Transportation Plan
(“RTP”).
The RTP is a set of policies, plans
and programs to guide the effective coordination and orderly
prpgraimning of transportation improvements among local, regional,
state and federal agencies.
Most local transportation projects
must be consistent with the RTP in order to obtain state, federal
or sales tax funding.
The RTP must contain an aviation element which sets forth the
current
SANDAG policies related to commercial aviation.
Generally,
in order to receive necessary federal funding from the
Federal Aviation Administration to study,
expand,
move, replace,
etc.
a commercial aviation airport, it must be appropriately
recognized as a possible commercial airport site in the RTP.
Currently, Lindbergh Field is designated in the RTP as the
region’s air carrier airport.
Other airport site options are also
described.
Later this year the board of directors will be asked to adopt
these or revised policies regarding the commercial airport.
It is
possible the policies could lead to an impact on the operation of
Lindbergh Field in the future.
2
This would include the
possibility of increasing,
reducing or eliminating noise impacts
of Lindbergh Field.
Mayor Bixler has three real property interests in the airport
influence area of Lindbergh Field.
Two of the interests are in
the area impacted by noise.
They fall within the
65
CNEL noise
contours.
The 65
CNEL threshold is the level established by the
State of California Adopted Noise Standards to identify the “Noise
Impact Boundary” of airports, that is,
a boundary within which the
noise environment is not suitable for residential land use.
Under
the Lindbergh Field Comprehensive Land Use Plan, residential uses
are allowed in the 65
CNEL only if specific conditions are met.
Property
3 is outside the noise contours.
All of the properties
are located beyond a 2,500 foot radius of the boundaries of
Lindbergh Field.
All of the properties are owned by Mayor Bixler
and are described as follows:
Property
1 is a triplex.
Each unit is currently rented.
Property
2 is a duplex.
Each unit is currently rented.
2
On October 14,
1993,
you notified me that a decision concerning
the RTP was scheduled for October 22,
1993.


Our File No.
A-93-347
Page
3
Property
3 has two commercial/industrial buildings.
One is
available for lease and the other is used by Mayor Bixler for
storage.
ANALYSIS
The Act prohibits a public official from making,
participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest.
(Section 87100.)
A public official has a financial interest in a decision if
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally,
on the official or a member of his or her
immediate family or on:
(b)
Any real property in which the public of
ficial has a direct or indirect interest worth one
thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income,
other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending institu
tion in the regular course of business on terms-s
available to the public without regard to official
status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to, received byor
promised to the public official within 12 months
prior to the time when the decision is made.
Section 87103(b)
and (c).
According to the information you have provided,
Mayor Bixler
has an ownership interest in three improved parcels of real
property.
Property 1,
a triplex,
is residential.
Each unit is
presently rented.
Property 2,
a duplex, is residential.
Each
unit is presently rented.
Property
3 has two commercial!
industrial buildings.
One is available for lease and the other is
used by Mayor Bixler for storage.
His financial interest in all
of these property holdings is greater than $1,000.
In addition,
because Mayor Bixler is currently leasing the
duplex and the triplex,
and may have leased one of the commercial!
industrial buildings in the past, his lessees are sources of
income to him.
Thus,
under the facts provided in your letter of
August 3,
1993 and in the additional documentation provided to
this office on October 14,
1993,
both Mayor Bixier’s interests in
real property and his lessees are potentially disqualifying
financial interests as defined in Section 87103.
As the Mayor of the City of Imperial Beach and a member of
SANDAG’s Board of Directors,
Michael Bixler is a public official.
(Section 82048.)
Thus,
Mayor Bixier must disqualify himself from
participating in any
SANDAG decision which will have a reasonably


our File No.
A—93—347
Page 4
foreseeable material financial effect on his interests in real
property, his lessees,
or on any other of his economic interests.
3
The question presented for our consideration is whether,
under the facts provided in your letter,
Mayor Bixler may
participate in SANDAG’s decisions concerning the RTP?
4
All three
of the properties identified in your letter are located beyond a
2,500 foot radius of Lindbergh Field, yet all are within the
airport influence area of Lindbergh Field.
Two of the properties
are situated within the Noise Impact Boundary of Lindbergh Field.
When property that is the subject of a governmental decision
is more than 2,500 feet away from a public official’s real
property interests, the effect of the decision generally is not
considered material unless:
(1)
There are specific circumstances
regarding the decision, its effect,
and the nature
of the real property in which the official has an
interest, which make it reasonably foreseeable that
the fair market value or the rental value of the
real property in which the official has an interest
will be affected by the amounts set forth in
subdivisions
(a) (3) (A)
or
(a) (3) (B) ;5 and
(2)
Either of the following apply:
(A)
The effect will not be substantially
the same as the effect upon at least 25
percent of all the properties which are within
During our telephone conversation of October 14,
1993,
you
informed me neither the tenants of Mayor Bixler’s properties nor
any other sources of income to Mayor Bixier of $250 or more during
the past 12 months would be financially affected by SANDAG’s
decisions concerning Lindbergh Field.
Accordingly,
our letter
only considers and analyzes Mayor Bixier’s real property holdings
situated in the airport. influence area of Lindbergh Field.
The Commission does not act as a finder of fact.
The advice
provided in this letter is applicable only to the extent that the
facts provided are correct and that all of the material facts have
been provided.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
71.)
Subdivisions
(a) (3) (A)
or
(a) (3) (B)
provide in pertinent part
that the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of $10,000 or more on the fair market value of the real
property in which the official has an interest or the decision
will affect the rental value of the property by $1,000 or more per
12 month period.
(Regulation 18702.3.)
Subdivision
(d)
provides
that in determining whether the decisions will have the effects
set forth in subdivisions
(a) (3) (A)
or
(B), the factors set forth
in subdivision (d),
among others,
shall be considered.


Our File No.
A—93-347
Page 5
a 2,500 foot radius
Of the boundaries of the
real property in which the official has an
interest; or
(B)
There are not at least 10 properties
under separate ownership within a 2,500 foot
radius of the property in which the official
has an interest.
(Regulation 18702.3(b).)
Under this regulation,
Mayor Bixler is permitted to
participate in S.ANDAG’s decision concerning the RTP unless the
value of Mayor Bixler’s real property interests situated in the
airport influence area of Lindbergh Field will be financially
affected by the dollar amounts specified in Regulation
18702.3(b) (1)
and either of the specific factors enumerated in
subdivisions
(b) (2) (A)
or
(B),
which make the effects on his
properties somewhat unique,
apply.
(See,
Diaz Advice Letter,
No.
A-89-425 and Heidt Advice Letter,
No.
A-90—245.)
We must leave to you and Mayor Bixier the factual
determination of the magnitude of the financial impact on each of
Mayor Bixler’s real property interests as a result of each
SANDAG
decision on the RTP and whether the impact falls within the
guidelines provided in Regulation 18702.3(b) (2).
If the
conclusion is that the decision will impact the fair market value
of Mayor Bixler’s properties by $10,000,
or the rental value by
$1,000 in a 12—month period,
Mayor Bixler will not be permitted to
participate in the RTP decision unless one of the requirements of
subdivision
(b) (2)
is satisfied.
As you have indicated in your letter, the effect of the
decision on Mayor Bixler’s properties will be substantially the
same as the effect upon at least
25% of all the properties which
are within a 2,500 foot radius of the boundaries of his three
properties.
This would appear to satisfy the requirement of
Regulation 18702.3(b)(2)(A).
In addition,
you state in your
letter that there are at least ten properties under separate
ownership within 2,500 feet of Lindbergh Field.
This would appear
to satisfy the requirement of Regulation 18702.3(b) (2) (B).


Our File No.
A-93-347
Page
6
I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you
requested.
If you have any further questions regarding this
matter, please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322_5901.6
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Deanne Stone
Senior Commission Counsel
6
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of lO per page.

(“RTP”)
for the San Diego
region contains an aviation element providing for the
consideration of aviation sites other than Lindbergh Field.
Mayor
Bixler has three real property interests located in the airport
influence area of Lindbergh Field that will be financially
affected by these decisions.
May Mayor Bixier participate in
S.ANDAG’s decisions concerning the RTP?

CONCLUSION: Mayor Bixier may participate in SANDAG’s decisions concerning
the RTP if the effect of those decisions on his real property
interests will be substantially the same as the effect upon at
least 25 percent of all the properties which are located within a
2,500 foot radius of the boundaries of his properties,
or there
are at least 10 properties under separate ownership within a 2,500
foot radius of Mayor Bixier’s properties.

regulations appear at

18000—18954.

Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.

QUESTION The Regional Transportation Plan (“RTP”) for the San Diego region contains an aviation element providing for the consideration of aviation sites other than Lindbergh Field. Mayor Bixler has three real property interests located in the airport influence area of Lindbergh Field that will be financially affected by these decisions. May Mayor Bixier participate in S.ANDAG’s decisions concerning the RTP? CONCLUSION Mayor Bixier may participate in SANDAG’s decisions concerning the RTP if the effect of those decisions on his real property interests will be substantially the same as the effect upon at least 25 percent of all the properties which are located within a 2,500 foot radius of the boundaries of his properties, or there are at least 10 properties under separate ownership within a 2,500 foot radius of Mayor Bixier’s properties. 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
